ISEN 432 Syllabus

Section 500 (55707)

Human Factors Engineering for Aerospace Designs

Spring 2026 - College Station

Course Information

Meeting Times:  Meeting Type: LEC

Meeting Days: TR

Start Time: 09:35AM

End Time: 10:50AM

Start Date: 01/12/2026

End Date: 05/05/2026

Meeting Location:  ZACH 342

Credit Hours:  3

Instructor Details

Nancy Currie-Gregg

Email:  currie-gregg@tamu.edu

Office Location:  ETB 4080, ZACH 510C

Phone:  979-458-2342

Office Hours

Monday: 12:00-1:00p, ETB 4080 | Wednesday: 5:00-6:00p (online)

Preferred Contact Method

Communication with the instructor throughout the semester is highly encouraged.

Students needing assistance should attend office hours with either the instructor or

GTA. Additionally, to provide timely responses to student questions and to deliver

announcements to the class quickly, an MS Teams site will be established and used

throughout the semester. All students must have access to MS Teams and accept

the invitation to join the course site. Communication via MS Teams is highly

preferred instead of email.

Any communications with the instructor that contain personal information must be

done via private chat through MS Teams. MS Teams chats/messages may also be

used for student-to-student communication or to post questions to the entire class

or subgroups.

Webpage:  https://safe.engr.tamu.edu

Biography

Dr. Nancy Currie-Gregg is the Director of the Texas A&M University Space Institute.

As the Don Lummus '58 Professor of Practice in Engineering, she holds joint

appointments in the Departments of Aerospace Engineering and Industrial and

Systems Engineering at Texas A&M University's College of Engineering.

Internationally recognized for her human-system integration, safety engineering,

and risk management expertise, her research focuses on optimizing human

performance and safety in aerospace systems. She also serves on the Board of

Directors for the Texas Space Commission, leading initiatives that promote growth

across all sectors of the Texas space economy.

Dr. Currie-Gregg spent the majority of her career supporting NASA's human

spaceflight programs. A former NASA astronaut, she flew on four space shuttle

missions, logging over 1,000 hours in space, and made significant contributions to

the development of human spaceflight operations. Following the Space Shuttle

across all sectors of the Texas space economy.

Dr. Currie-Gregg spent the majority of her career supporting NASA's human

spaceflight programs. A former NASA astronaut, she flew on four space shuttle

missions, logging over 1,000 hours in space, and made significant contributions to

the development of human spaceflight operations. Following the Space Shuttle

Columbia tragedy, she managed the Space Shuttle Program Safety and Mission

Assurance Office, overseeing the agency's return-to-flight efforts. She later served

as Principal Engineer and Chief Engineer with the NASA Engineering and Safety

Center, where she led independent engineering assessments for high-risk human

spaceflight initiatives. A retired U.S. Army Colonel and Master Army Aviator, she

accrued more than 4,000 flight hours in both rotary- and fixed-wing aircraft.

Dr. Currie-Gregg holds a Bachelor's degree in Biological Sciences with honors from

The Ohio State University, a Master of Science in Safety Engineering from the

University of Southern California, and a Ph.D. in Industrial Engineering from the

University of Houston.

Socials

https://www.linkedin.com/in/curriegregg/

Catalog Description

Human Factors Engineering for Aerospace Designs. (3-0). Credit 3. Physiological and

psychophysiological issues encountered in aviation and space environments and

their effect on human cognitive and physical performance; survey methods for

human workload and performance evaluations; apply human-systems design

principles to an aerospace design project. Prerequisites: Junior or senior

classification. Cross-listed with AERO 436.

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  Junior or senior classification. Cross Listing:

AERO 436/ISEN 432.

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

Explain how aspects of human sensory, motor, and cognitive attributes may

influence human performance in the operation of aviation and space

systems.

Identify principal physiological and psychophysiological issues related to

aviation and spaceflight environments and describe their potential impact on

human performance.

Review published literature on physiological and psychophysiological issues

and evaluate proposed mitigations and countermeasures.

Identify human-system integration challenges for aerospace applications.

Review and interpret specific human-system integration requirements such

as ergonomics, physical/cognitive workload, attention, situational awareness,

and mission performance to develop aircraft or spacecraft system designs.

Describe methods for principled human-in-the-loop evaluations.

Special Course Designation

None

This is a cross-listed course with both AERO and ISEN sections due to the

Review and interpret specific human-system integration requirements such

as ergonomics, physical/cognitive workload, attention, situational awareness,

and mission performance to develop aircraft or spacecraft system designs.

Describe methods for principled human-in-the-loop evaluations.

Special Course Designation

None

This is a cross-listed course with both AERO and ISEN sections due to the

interdisciplinary nature of the subject. It is also stacked with the graduate versions

of the course (AERO 636/ISEN 632). Graduate and undergraduate students will

have different requirements and weighting for quizzes, homework assignments,

and the course project.

Grading Policy

Grading Policy

Performance in the course will be evaluated through individual activities and a

group term project. Below is a summary of the course requirements and their

relative contribution to the final course grade.

Course evaluation methods

Method of evaluation

Percent of grade

Lecture activities

10%

Quizzes (4)

30%

Homework

30%

Project (Interim deliverables 45%, final deliverables

55%)

30%

Grading Scale (based on weighted total score):

A = 89.5-100%; B = 79.5-89.4%; C = 69.5-79.4%; D = 59.5-69.4%; F =  ≤  59.4%

Lecture Activities

Class attendance and participation are the individual responsibility of each student.

Students are expected to attend all lectures and participate in the learning process

unless they have a university-approved absence (see  Student Rule 7 ).

This course will use active learning methods, such as group activities, discussions,

surveys, and experiential learning, to encourage student engagement in their

learning. The points for each lecture activity will vary, with those associated with

experiential learning opportunities having the most points.

All lecture activities must be completed in class and may not be submitted late

unless the instructor has granted an extension due to a pre-approved university-

surveys, and experiential learning, to encourage student engagement in their

learning. The points for each lecture activity will vary, with those associated with

experiential learning opportunities having the most points.

All lecture activities must be completed in class and may not be submitted late

unless the instructor has granted an extension due to a pre-approved university-

excused absence. If a student misses an activity due to a university-excused

absence (see  Student Rule 7 ), and has notified the instructor, in writing, in advance

of the absence, they may receive an extension to the lecture activity submission

deadline.

Quizzes

There will be four quizzes throughout the semester. The detailed schedule denotes

what material will be covered on each quiz.

Homework

There will be approximately five homework assignments during the semester.

Detailed instructions for each homework assignment, including due dates and

times and the maximum possible points for each assignment element, will be

provided on the course Canvas site and discussed during lectures.

Some homework assignments will also be designated as “design competitions.”

Students who present superior solutions for the assignment will be awarded extra

credit points, which will be applied to the homework grade.

Course Project

A crucial aspect of the course is participation in a group project to design an aircraft

or spacecraft system that enhances operator effectiveness, usability, and safety.

This project aims to deepen comprehension and understanding by applying course

concepts and methods to create systems that account for human capabilities and

limitations in aerospace environments.

The instructor will form teams based on each student's assessment of their

interests and capabilities. Each team will select an aerospace human-system design

project (e.g., a situational awareness display, rover controller, personal restraint

system, payload workstation, etc.) and research the need for a solution, develop a

proposed design, construct a prototype, and test or evaluate the prototype using

the techniques and methods discussed in this course.

There will be several interim deliverables throughout the semester to ensure

progress on the project. These include a design concept of operations, HSI design

requirements, and human-in-the-loop testing and evaluation criteria. The final

deliverables for the course project are a prototype with associated design

documentation and an oral presentation by the project team. All team members

must attend and participate in the presentation. Additionally, there will be an award

for the best design, as determined by an expert panel. The due dates for all

requirements, and human-in-the-loop testing and evaluation criteria. The final

deliverables for the course project are a prototype with associated design

documentation and an oral presentation by the project team. All team members

must attend and participate in the presentation. Additionally, there will be an award

for the best design, as determined by an expert panel. The due dates for all

deliverables are provided in the detailed course schedule.

Details regarding specific formats and requirements for all course project

deliverables will be covered during lectures and posted on Canvas. Additionally,

detailed grading rubrics for all deliverables will be provided and posted well in

advance of the due dates. All students in the group will receive the same score on

the project, unless indicated otherwise based on peer evaluations.

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

Homework and project assignments submitted after the due date/time will not

receive total credit, unless the delay is due to a university-excused absence (see

Student Rule 7). Points will be deducted for assignments submitted past the due

date and time on Canvas—equivalent to 20% of the total points possible for each

day, or portion thereof, past the due date. No late homework or project

assignments will be accepted after 4 days.

Lecture activities may not be submitted late unless an extension has been granted

due to a pre-approved university-excused absence.

Course Schedule

A detailed course schedule, including homework assignment and project

deliverable due dates is provided in Appendix A and posted on Canvas. Any

updates to the schedule will be announced during lectures and through Canvas

announcements.

Course topics:

Aerospace health and human performance risks

Aerospace environments: Physical and cognitive stressors

Aerospace human-system design process, including safety considerations

Anthropometric considerations for spacecraft and aircraft

Habitability considerations for long-duration space missions

Cognitive workload, fatigue, attention, and situational awareness

Aerospace vehicle designs: Displays & controls

Physical/cognitive workload evaluation: physiological and subjective

measures

Human performance & usability evaluations

Human reliability measures and human error mitigation strategies

Human-system interface design for Extravehicular activity (EVA)

Additional Course Information

Other resources:

The detailed lecture schedule, supplemental reading and reference material, lecture

slides, and all homework and project assignments will be posted on course Canvas

site. Reading the referenced material prior to viewing the lectures is essential in

order to be prepared to effectively participate in class discussions.

Discussion forums

Discussion forums for various topics will be provided on MS Teams will be used as a

means for student collaborations and discussions.

Course material and copyright:

All materials used in this course are copyrighted. “Materials” includes all items

produced for this course, such as the syllabus, detailed course schedule, lecture

slides, on-line lecture videos, homework or exam questions and solutions, and

additional resources posted on the course site by the instructor.

You may not copy or distribute course materials, unless expressly granted

permission by the author. Posting course materials on education technology

company websites (e.g., Chegg, Course Hero, etc.) is expressly prohibited. Violations

of this course policy will be treated as academic misconduct in accordance with

Aggie Honor System Office policies.

Re-grading Policy

Students have three days following the release of grades for an assignment or quiz

to submit a re-grade request. The regrading request must be submitted in writing

(not to exceed 1 page with 11-point font and single spacing). The request must

clearly indicate the relevant problem(s) with the grade and justification for why re-

grading is warranted.

AI Statement

The primary goal of this course is for students to understand the effects of

aerospace environments on human health and performance, and to formulate

design approaches to mitigate these effects. Judicious and ethical use of Artificial

Intelligence (AI) tools can assist with initial comprehension and practice of course

concepts. Limited use of AI is therefore permitted to support student learning, but

not to replace a student’s original work.

aerospace environments on human health and performance, and to formulate

design approaches to mitigate these effects. Judicious and ethical use of Artificial

Intelligence (AI) tools can assist with initial comprehension and practice of course

concepts. Limited use of AI is therefore permitted to support student learning, but

not to replace a student’s original work.

Examples of  permitted use  of AI agents include the following:

To assist with comprehension of course material or concepts

The use of AI-based tools to further explore concepts presented in the

course is encouraged. For example, AI tools may be used to provide

alternative explanations of concepts presented in lecture materials or

textbooks. AI may be used as a study aid, similar to tutoring, but not to

generate material that is submitted for academic credit.

To assist with the readability of homework or project assignments

Use of AI tools to revise and edit a student’s own writing to improve clarity,

organization, or readability is permitted. However, using an AI tool to

generate responses, analyses, calculations, or written content for homework

or project assignments is not permitted, even if the output is subsequently

modified.

Instructions for each homework or project assignment will specify whether and how

AI-based tools may be used for that assignment. If any AI tools are used at any

stage during completion of an assignment, students must properly cite the use of AI

and submit the following information with the assignment:

The name of the AI tool and the specific version used

A brief explanation of why assistance from the AI tool was sought

Any prompts used, provided exactly as input to the tool, enclosed in

quotation marks.

Students should be aware that AI-generated results may be inaccurate, incomplete,

or biased. Students are personally responsible for verifying the accuracy of any

information obtained from AI tools. Additionally, copyrighted materials (including

lecture materials) and personal or sensitive information should not be shared

unintentionally when interacting with AI agents.

Use of AI assistants during any portion of a quiz is strictly prohibited .

Any use of AI assistance during a quiz will be considered academic misconduct

under Rule 20.1.2.2.1 (“Cheating”) and a violation of the Aggie Honor Code. See the

Aggie Honor System Office for additional information.

Questions regarding citation, academic integrity, or acceptable use of AI in this

course should be directed to the instructor  prior to  the assignment submission.
